title: Directional Cluster Migration Driven by Escape-Rate Asymmetry in Multi-Compartment Granular Systems
authors: Kai Kono, Hiroyuki Ebata, Shio Inagaki
abstract: Granular materials are inherently out-of-equilibrium systems due to energy dissipation through inelastic collisions and friction. When driven by mechanical agitation such as vibration, they exhibit rich collective behaviors including segregation, clustering, and spontaneous oscillations. Here, we report directional stepwise migration of particle clusters from one compartment to the next in a vertically vibrated granular system composed of small and large particles. To clarify the underlying mechanism, we directly measured how the flux of both particle species depends on the instantaneous particle populations. The measurements reveal an asymmetric interaction between particle species: the flux of small particles is enhanced by the presence of large particles, whereas that of large particles is suppressed by small particles. A minimal flux model incorporating these measured fluxes reproduces the observed directional dynamics and provides an experimentally grounded framework for collective transport in vibrated granular systems.
summary: The paper investigates how particle clusters move directionally between compartments in a vibrated granular system. By measuring species-specific fluxes, the authors show that an asymmetry in how small and large particles interact drives this migration. This work provides an experimental basis for modeling collective transport in driven, dissipative systems.
main_problem: Understanding the mechanism behind the directional stepwise migration of particle clusters in a multi-compartment vibrated granular system.
main_result: The study identifies an asymmetric interaction between particle species where large particles enhance small particle flux, while small particles suppress large particle flux, driving directional transport.
method: Direct measurement of particle species flux as a function of instantaneous populations, followed by the implementation of a minimal flux model.
model_or_system: A vertically vibrated granular system composed of small and large particles distributed across multiple compartments.
key_observables: Flux of small and large particle species, particle populations per compartment, and directional migration dynamics.
important_parameters: Mechanical agitation (vibration), particle size distribution, and instantaneous particle populations.
main_assumptions: The system is treated as an out-of-equilibrium system driven by energy dissipation through inelastic collisions and friction.
paper_structure: Introduction to granular out-of-equilibrium dynamics, experimental report on directional cluster migration, flux measurement analysis, and validation via a minimal flux model.
why_interesting: The discovery of asymmetric, population-dependent flux and directional transport in a non-equilibrium system provides insights into collective transport and driven-dissipative dynamics relevant to open quantum systems.
